task_context.question: Where is the official residence of the head of state of Germany?
task_context.agent_role: Produce one compact second-hop retrieval query targeting the missing bridge or supporting evidence.

visible_agent_inputs.question: Where is the official residence of the head of state of Germany?
visible_agent_inputs.summary_1:
Official residence of Germany's head of state (Federal President)
- Hammerschmidt Villa: "served as the primary official seat and primary official residence" of the President from 1950 until 1994.
- Bellevue Palace in Berlin: President Richard von Weizsäcker "made Bellevue Palace in Berlin his primary official seat and residence in 1994"; since 1994 Hammerschmidt Villa is a "secondary official seat and secondary official residence".
- Missing bridge fact: none — passages explicitly link the Federal President (head of state) to Bellevue Palace in Berlin; no additional retrieval needed. Optional next hops (if verification desired): 1) "Bellevue Palace" — lede/infobox and "History" section; 2) "Hammerschmidt Villa" — lede/infobox and "History" section.

input_dict.current_prompt:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Priority rules for ambiguity and disambiguation:
- If summary_1 lists multiple candidate anchors or otherwise signals ambiguity, emit a single connector-style query that concatenates the prioritized anchor tokens (use the order or priority visible in summary_1) and then append exactly one short attribute token or a visible year token for disambiguation.
- If summary_1 contains a comma-separated list of individuals and the missing evidence concerns a member, prefer the earliest-listed individual token paired with the visible group token and append the single attribute token member.
- If the question contains a visible year token that disambiguates a title, include that year token before the attribute token in the query.
- If none of the above applies, emit a single-anchor query using the most relevant visible token and at most one short attribute token when the question implies an attribute.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.

retrieved_doc_snippets: Bellevue Palace (Germany) | Bellevue Palace (German: "Schloss Bellevue" ), located in Berlin's Tiergarten district, has been the official residence of the President of Germany since 1994. It is situated on the banks of the Spree river, near the Berlin Victory Column, along the northern edge of the Großer Tiergarten park. Its namethe French for "beautiful view"derives from its scenic prospect over the Spree's course.

Hotel Bellevue Palace | The Bellevue Palace is a five-star luxury hotel located in the Old City of Bern, Switzerland. Owned by the Swiss Confederation, it is the state's guesthouse for visiting heads of state and government, and is host to dozens of members of parliament during the weeks the assembly is in session.

Bellevue Palace, Kassel | Bellevue Palace (German: Palais Bellevue or Schloss Bellevue) in Kassel was built in 1714 for Charles I, Landgrave of Hesse-Kassel. Originally the building served as an Observatory. It became a residence, and then part of Bellevue Castle, which was later destroyed. Today the building houses a museum devoted to the Brothers Grimm.

Berlin Bellevue station | Bellevue is a railway station on the Berlin Stadtbahn in the Hansaviertel district of Berlin, Germany. It is located on the elevated Stadtbahn line and served by Berlin S-Bahn trains. The station is named after nearby Bellevue Palace, the residence of the President of Germany.

Bellevue High School (Bellevue, Washington) | Bellevue High School is a public secondary school located in Bellevue, Washington. In the 2012–2013 school year, Bellevue had 1421 students enrolled in grades 9 through 12.
Bellevue railway station, Perth | Bellevue railway station (also known as Bellevue Junction) was a junction station located on the Eastern Railway in Bellevue, Western Australia.
Nebraska Medicine- Bellevue | Nebraska Medicine- Bellevue (Bellevue Medical Center) is a hospital located in Bellevue, Sarpy County, Nebraska, United States. It opened on May 17, 2010.
source_state.summary_2: {"summary":"Bellevue Palace in Berlin has been the official residence of the President of Germany (the Federal President) since 1994. Bellevue Palace is located in Berlin’s Tiergarten district on the banks of the Spree river near the Victory Column."}

output.query: Politics of Berlin President of Germany Where is the official residence of the head of state of Germany?

retrieved_doc_snippets: Politics of Berlin | Berlin is a city-state and the capital of the Federal Republic of Germany. The President of Germany, whose functions are primarily ceremonial under the German constitution, has his official residence in Schloss Bellevue. Berlin is the seat of the German executive, housed in the Chancellery (the "Bundeskanzleramt"). Facing the Chancellery is the Bundestag, the German Parliament, housed in the renovated Reichstag building since the government's move to Berlin in 1998. The Bund ...[truncated]

Capital of Germany | The capital of Germany is the city state of Berlin. It is the seat of the President of Germany, whose official residence is Schloss Bellevue. The Bundesrat ("federal council") is the representation of the Federal States ("Bundesländer") of Germany and has its seat at the former Prussian Herrenhaus (House of Lords). Though most of the ministries are seated in Berlin, some of them, as well as some minor departments, are seated in Bonn, the former capital of West Germany.

Hammerschmidt Villa | Hammerschmidt Villa (German: "Villa Hammerschmidt" ) is a villa located in the German city of Bonn that served as the primary official seat and primary official residence of the President of the Federal Republic of Germany from 1950 until 1994. President Richard von Weizsäcker made Bellevue Palace in Berlin his primary official seat and residence in 1994. Since 1994, the Hammerschmidt Villa has served as a secondary official seat and secondary official residence for the Pre ...[truncated]

Governing Mayor of Berlin | The Governing Mayor (German: "Regierender Bürgermeister" ) of Berlin is the head of government, presiding over the Berlin Senate. As Berlin is an independent city as well as one of the constituent States of Germany ("Bundesländer"), the office is the equivalent of the Ministers-President of the other German states —except the two other city-states of Hamburg and Bremen, where the heads of government are called "First Mayor" and "Mayor and President of the Senate", res ...[truncated]

President of Germany | The President of Germany, officially the President of the Federal Republic of Germany (German: "Bundespräsident der Bundesrepublik Deutschland" ), is the head of state of Germany.

Bellevue Palace (Germany) | Bellevue Palace (German: "Schloss Bellevue" ), located in Berlin's Tiergarten district, has been the official residence of the President of Germany since 1994. It is situated on the banks of the Spree river, near the Berlin Victory Column, along the northern edge of the Großer Tiergarten park. Its namethe French for "beautiful view"derives from its scenic prospect over the Spree's course.

Berlin Bellevue station | Bellevue is a railway station on the Berlin Stadtbahn in the Hansaviertel district of Berlin, Germany. It is located on the elevated Stadtbahn line and served by Berlin S-Bahn trains. The station is named after nearby Bellevue Palace, the residence of the President of Germany.
target_state.summary_2: The official residence of the head of state of Germany is Bellevue Palace in Berlin. Hammerschmidt Villa served as the President's primary official residence in Bonn from 1950 to 1994 and has been a secondary official residence since 1994.
